dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a traffic incident and chest pain at Southeast end of Airport Road.
Hi, I'm Stephanie, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having chest pain and can barely breathe since the crash.
Can you tell me your name and address, sir?
My name is Abram... address is 8813 Lightburn Place, [CITY].
How old are you, Mr. Abram?
37 years old. I was driving my truck when the car hit me from the side head-on.
Describe what you mean by chest pain, please.
It's sharp in the middle of my chest, and it hurts more when I breathe deep.
Mr. Abram, can you squeeze my hands tight for me, left one first and then the right one? Please do it now when I say GO.
GO!
Can you take deep and slow breaths for me now? Don't worry, I won't make you do anything uncomfortable. Okay?
Hmm, okay. I'll try... though it's hard to breathe fully right now.
Are you experiencing any difficulty breathing in terms of your effort, or is it just painful, Abram? How does it feel like when you inhale?
No, I'm not gasping for air. It's shallow and slow. It's just very painful to breathe deeply.
We need to take your pulse and examine your skin. Your color seems okay, and skin is warm and dry.
You said you were involved in a car crash. Can you tell me how it happened and when, Mr. Abram?
I was in my car, alone, sir. My car was struck on my driver side with significant damage. I was trapped for about 26 minutes until they could extract me.

When your car was un-stuck from the accident, can you feel your extremities clearly before and after the incident? Did you consume any medication or alcoholic beverages while you were driving before this incident?
I had clear sensation in both arms and legs before the crash, and nothing numb or cold. I did not drink or take medication. I have asthma as a kid, but nothing recently.
When was the last time you ate something before this incident, Mr. Abram?
I'm Mrs. Dunne, I am his work colleague and was the one who called 911. He had not eaten for eight hours before he started his shift.
Where do you want to be taken to?
I don't know. Just take me to the closest trauma center.
How would you rate your chest pain from 0, which is no pain, to 10, the worst pain you've ever felt?
Oh, I would say it's a 10. Constant and I can't get comfortable.
Does it feel better when you're resting with no movement or does the backboard affect it?
Yes, lying still seems better. The most painful part is the left side.
Mr. Abram, I'm going to check your skin for any rashes, cuts or bruises. Does that feel okay with you?
I found a small laceration on the left side of your face, under your eye. It has been controlled now. Is there any rash or suspected insect bite?
I don't feel anything, just the pain in my chest and some tenderness around my lower back. I didn't get any insect bite though.
I want to shine a light into your eyes now. Do you mind?
Go ahead, I can be tested like this. I've seen it on videos before.
Your pupils are equal and reactive. Do you have any pain or discomfort in the head or neck?
No neck pain except for the left shoulder blade area. The pain has been here ever since I was pulled out.
I need to listen to your lungs and heart with a stethoscope. Okay?
Your lungs sound clear but shallow. Do you wheeze at all?
No wheezing. My heart is beating fast but I don't feel anxious.
I'm going to gently press your abdomen to check for tenderness. This might be uncomfortable.
Okay, press away.
You have a soft abdomen. You moan during palpation. Any nausea, vomiting, or blood in your stool?
No, just abdominal moaning when you pressed my sternum. I was on a water fast this week before the accident.
Pulse is 80 beats per minute and regular.
Respirations are 12 breaths per minute and shallow.
Blood pressure is 130 over 88.
SpO2 is 97 percent on room air.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
We are going to place you in a cervical collar now. Please stay still.
Go ahead, but the collar causes some discomfort.
Mr. Abram, we need to perform full spinal immobilization. That means you must lie still and stay extended on the backboard.
We will now perform a 4-lead ECG monitoring and later obtain a 12-lead ECG.
We need to place an IV in your arm to deliver fluids.
We successfully placed two IVs with normal saline at KVO.
You were an unrestrained driver when the collision happened.
We are fully restricting your spinal motion as part of trauma precautions.
Your peripheral pulses are strong and present bilaterally.
Based on your symptoms and EKG results, we're following protocol 4-1 for general trauma management.
Pulse is 90, respirations 16, blood pressure is 121 over 84, and SpO2 is 98 percent.
Both IVs are active, and EKG remains in sinus rhythm.
Is your pain any different in intensity or location?
No, it's still the same. Does the collar affect the wound on my left side?
Let me check it quickly. It seems the collar is not interfering with the wound.
You're safe and secure on the backboard. Radio, we're en route to [COMPANY_NAME], a trauma center.
[COMPANY_NAME], do you copy?

We've turned over the mobile patient to the trauma team at [CITY]. Vital signs from on scene and during transit: first set at 80, 12, 130/88, 97 percent; second set at 90, 16, 121/84, 98 percent. He has altered mental status but is lucid when appropriate. Glucose unavailable. Patient has mild asthma history.
